text: The camera port is visibly positioned, providing a view into the surgical field for the nephrectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned in a lateral decubitus position with the left side down and the right side up, facilitating access to the surgical site.
The surgeon places a left-sided operating port in a fixed position, visible in the lateral view of the patient.
A 12mm operating port is positioned three finger-breadths superior and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine on the patient's right side.
The surgeon places a fixed port in the midclavicular line just below the costal margin, establishing an access point for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon considers using a 12mm port instead of the usual 5mm port for the left-sided operating position, as seen in the laparoscopic setup.
The camera is positioned in a triangulated manner between the two previously placed ports, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection by making an incision in the posterior peritoneum, just lateral to the reflection of the mesocolon, to enter the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon makes a superficial incision through the peritoneum, ensuring it does not penetrate deeper tissues.
The surgeon avoids transgressing the Gerota's fascia to prevent complications in retracting the colon and to facilitate further dissection.
The initial incision is visible on the anterior surface of the lower pole of the kidney, rather than along the white line of Toldt.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to incise at the point of reflection of the mesocolon, visible at the tip of the instrument.
The surgeon decides against making a further lateral incision, maintaining the current surgical approach within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision along the edge of the liver, allowing the liver to fall forward due to gravity, minimizing the need for a fourth port.
I wouldn't routinely place a fourth port at the early stages of surgery because the position, if one is required, can be dependent upon the requirements.
It may be that a requirement for a liver retractor would put a fourth port in a different site to one required to retract the kidney or provide additional assistance.
The surgeon retracts the lower pole of the kidney laterally after dividing the peritoneal layer, applying tension to separate the peritoneum from the fascia overlying Girota's fat.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide minor adhesions at the site of the peritoneal incision.
The surgeon uses the dominant right hand to perform the majority of the dissection, while the left hand functions as a retractor, stabilizing the tissue during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, creating an open space between the lower pole and the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon incises Girota's fascia, exposing the duodenum and gonadal vein, with the ureter visible laterally.
The surgeon searches for the ureter, which is located lateral to the gonadal vein, using careful dissection techniques.
This reduces the risk of putting retraction on the gonadal vein and accidentally detaching it from the cava.
At the apex of the dissection triangle, the ureter becomes visible as the surgeon continues the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and carefully places an instrument beneath it, lifting the ureter to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon applies clips to the ureter and then divides it, ensuring secure closure before transection.
Providing we've approached the ureter lateral to the gonadal vein, there should lie nothing beyond the ureter and the abdominal wall.
The surgeon incises the Girota's fascia, retracting the Girota's fat along with the kidney towards the lateral wall.
The surgeon ensures that the Girota's fat remains attached to the kidney, maintaining its role as an oncological barrier during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the lateral peritoneal attachments of the kidney, ensuring the kidney remains stable and does not fall medially.

With effective retraction from the assistant's left hand, the surgeon frees the lateral margin of the kidney, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues at this early stage of the nephrectomy.
Indeed, I think this assists with the approach to the renal hilum.
The surgeon proceeds to identify the renal vessels, focusing on the anatomical structures within the surgical field.

The surgeon lifts the lower pole of the kidney superiorly and laterally, creating tension on the tissue within the triangle bordered by the lower margin of the renal vein and the lateral aspect of the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The harmonic scalpel is used to gently stroke the edge of the right renal vein, freeing adherent tissue in an atraumatic manner.
The surgeon searches for the renal artery located behind the right renal vein, using a stroking technique along the vein's side wall to expose the artery.
The surgeon attempts to locate the renal artery, expected to be positioned approximately the distance of the instrument's tip behind the renal vein, but the artery is not easily visible in this case.
The surgeon incises Girota's fascia to expose the anterior surface of the renal vein, with the incision extending along the liver margin.
The surgeon identifies a small knuckle of tissue just above the superior edge of the right renal vein, revealing the renal artery.

The surgeon identifies a second large branch of the renal vein, noting its common origin on the inferior vena cava (IVC) with two large early branches. The renal artery is visibly protruding between these two branches.
The surgeon continues to divide the Girota's fascia above the renal vein, enhancing the mobility of the renal hilum while carefully avoiding the superior portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon explores behind the renal vein, cleaning the fascia overlying the veins to safely approach and mobilize the renal artery between the vessels.
The surgeon prepares to divide the renal artery located behind the renal vein, aiming to approach the artery from behind the inferior vena cava (IVC) during a right-sided donor nephrectomy.

The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to carefully divide adhesions between the renal vessels, taking care to avoid traumatic injury to the vein by ensuring the instrument does not penetrate the vein's edge.
The surgeon applies locking clips to one of the two renal veins, preparing to divide it while ensuring the other vein will continue to drain the kidney.
The surgeon applies the first clip successfully to the renal vein, but the second clip fails to lock due to potential tissue obstruction or improper loading in the clipper plier.
The surgeon places two locking clips on the patient side of the renal vein and one clip on the specimen side, ensuring secure occlusion of the vein.

The surgeon divides the vein, providing access to the renal artery located behind the residual renal vein. The surgeon gently strokes the fat overlying the artery to free any adhesions without causing trauma.
The surgeon uses a suction device to perform atraumatic dissection, carefully removing tissue without cutting, which helps avoid unnecessary trauma during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue on the lateral aspect of the artery, which is adherent to the residual renal vein. The medial aspect of the artery remains partially obscured.
The surgeon has exposed the renal artery and created a space between the artery and the vein. A linear stapler is applied to the artery, placing three rows of staples without cutting the vessel.

The surgeon applies a locking clip medially to the renal artery, ensuring vascular control. Staples are visible on the artery, and an additional clip is placed on the renal side before the artery is divided.
The surgeon tests for back filling of the renal vein by clamping it, observing no obvious filling. The renal artery is then divided lateral to the staple line.
The surgeon cuts a small vasoarterium located just beyond the renal artery, maintaining retraction to ensure a clear view for applying appropriate clips to secure the vessel.

The surgeon divides any residual main vessels to access a difficult bleeding point, assessing the volume of bleeding. If the bleeding is not threatening, the surgeon continues dissection to gain better control over the bleeding point.

Three locking clips are applied to the residual renal vein, which is then divided between the lateral-most clips. A single clip controls the small bleeding vasoarterium, allowing the surgeon to proceed with dissection of the upper pole of the kidney.

It's important when seeing bleeding to remain calm and to maintain a good view. Retraction is important, as is the position of the camera. It's tempting on behalf of the cameraman should the camera become splashed with blood to immediately remove the camera for cleaning. I would always discourage that and wash the camera with the suction irrigation. And that's something that we will always choose to do rather than remove the camera. This maintains the continuity of the surgery.

The surgeon identifies a bleeding point and decides on the method of control, opting to avoid using a harmonic scalpel or diathermy for delicate vessels like veins. Instead, a clear view is maintained, and a clip is applied to control the bleeding effectively.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the side wall of a renal vein by applying metallic clips in a line, resembling a picket fence, to control the bleeding. For more extensive bleeding, a large clip or a linear stapler may be used, allowing assessment of the vessels before division.

You'll note that this surgery has been performed without the need for a fourth port and a fourth port is rare, providing good retraction is applied with the left hand. The position of the camera port is very dependent on the body mass index of the patient and I would certainly ignore the umbilicus as a landmark.

The surgeon positions the camera port at a third point of a triangle formed by the iliac fossa port and the midclavicular subcostal port, adjusting for an obese patient with a large fatty apron by placing it laterally on the abdominal wall.

The surgeon places the camera port first using a Hassan cut-down technique under direct vision. After detaching the kidney from the lateral abdominal wall, the hilum is inspected, and any residual back bleeding is aspirated.
This includes feeding on the evening after surgery. mobilisation on the evening after surgery and active encouragement to dress and be mobile. We aim for discharge the day after their procedure.
The surgeon places an endoscopic bag within the abdomen and scoops the kidney into the bag, preparing to deliver it through an extension of the iliac fossa incision.
The surgeon closes the surgical wounds using standard suturing techniques, marking the completion of the nephrectomy procedure.
